coredata.dc:title: High-performance disease diagnosis fluorescent probe based on new type structure YbF3: Er3+@SiO2@GQDs
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Zhen
Jia, Mochen
Fu, Zuoling
Zhang, Mingxuan
Wang, Huayao
Xu, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Due to the difference of temperature and Iron (Fe3+) contents between cancer cells and normal cells, a simple disease diagnosis fluorescent probe integrated with sensitive thermometer and accurate ions detector is constructed based on YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs (YbSG) core–shell structure. The thermal sensing behaviors of YbF3: Er3+ are investigated by fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) technology using 4S3/2 and 4F9/2 levels of Er3+ ions irradiated by 980 nm laser. High Yb3+ doped concentrations induce strong energy back transfer (EBT) process between Er3+ and Yb3+, which not only tunes light emission but also effects temperature sensing property by population re-distribution in 4S3/2 and 4F9/2 levels of Er3+ ions. Additionally, fluorescence quenching of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can reflect Fe3+ ions contents in the range of 0–100 μM excited by 365 nm. The results display that Er3+ doped YbF3 combined with GQDs by introducing an intermediate medium silica layer has a potential application prospect for ultra-sensitive temperature measuring and detection of Fe3+ ions in the field of bio-medicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensitive thermometer
FIR technology
Energy back transfer process
Fluorescence quenching
Detection of Fe3+ ions

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Up-conversion luminescence properties and temperature sensing performance

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of synthesis; XRD patterns and SEM images of Er3+ and Yb3+ co-doped GdF3 nano-phosphors (b), (h) and micro-phosphors (e), (i); YF3 nano-phosphors (c), (j) and micro-phosphors (f), (k); YbF3 nano-phosphors (d), (l) and micro-phosphors (g), (m).

Emission spectra of GdF3/YF3: 1% Er3+, 5% Yb3+ and YbF3: 1% Er3+ nano/micro-phosphors (a) and (b); insets include integrated emission intensities of Er3+ ions; (c) up-conversion mechanisms of energy transfer, cross relaxation and energy back transfer between Er3+ and Yb3+ ions.

Temperature-dependent (a) UCL mappings of LnF3 (Ln = Gd, Y, Yb) nano-phosphors (left) and micro-phosphors (right) from top to bottom; FIR (I527 /I543 ) value, Sa and Sr of LnF3 nano-phosphors (b) and micro-phosphors (c).
(a) The plot of FIR (I655/I543 ) versus temperature, (b) the Sa and Sr , (c) δT, (d-e) the reliability of YbF3: 1% Er3+ nano-/micro-phosphors.

Scheme of the synthesis (a) and the XRD patterns (b) of YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs, corresponding TEM images of (c) YbF3, (d) GQDs; insert: size distributions, (e) YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2, (f) YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs together with HRTEM image of GQDs (g).

(a) Detection system showing temperature and Fe3+ ions sensing measurement of YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs in aqueous solution; (b) temperature-dependent integrated luminescence intensities at 655 and 543 nm emissions and calibration curves; (c) the Sa and Sr in a physiological temperature range (20–60 °C), (d) the δT, (e) the reliability of the sample; (f) optical photos of YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs solutions containing different ions (blank, Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Zn2+) under visible light, 365 nm and 980 nm excitation, corresponding to down-conversion emission spectra (g) and UC emission spectra (h).

Emission spectra of YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs excited at 365 nm (a) and 980 nm (b) with different Fe3+ concentrations (10 μM, 20 μM, 40 μM, 60 μM, 100 μM); (c) plot of the relativefluorescence intensity(F0/F-1) and concentration ([Fe3+]) for samples; (d) comparison of presented system with other reported materials for Fe3+ ions detection based on KSV and LOD; (e) cell viabilities after incubated with YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs for 48 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2.
Volume and molecules of unit cells, doping concentration of rare earth ions and distance between two ions of GdF3, YF3 and YbF3 nano-/micro-phosphors.
The comparison of maximum value of temperature sensitivity in some materials.

head.ce:title: High-performance disease diagnosis fluorescent probe based on new type structure YbF3: Er3+@SiO2@GQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory, Key Laboratory of Physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries, College of Physics, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory
Key Laboratory of Physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Coherent Light and Atomic and Molecular Spectroscopy Laboratory, Key Laboratory of Physics and Technology for Advanced Batteries, College of Physics, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional disease diagnosis fluorescent probe with high-sensitive temperature monitor and detection of Fe3+ ions, YbF3: Er3+@SiO2@GQDs, is designed based on strong EBT process and quenching effect of GQDs.

Due to the difference of temperature and Iron (Fe3+) contents between cancer cells and normal cells, a simple disease diagnosis fluorescent probe integrated with sensitive thermometer and accurate ions detector is constructed based on YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs (YbSG) core–shell structure. The thermal sensing behaviors of YbF3: Er3+ are investigated by fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) technology using 4S3/2 and 4F9/2 levels of Er3+ ions irradiated by 980 nm laser. High Yb3+ doped concentrations induce strong energy back transfer (EBT) process between Er3+ and Yb3+, which not only tunes light emission but also effects temperature sensing property by population re-distribution in 4S3/2 and 4F9/2 levels of Er3+ ions. Additionally, fluorescence quenching of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can reflect Fe3+ ions contents in the range of 0–100 μM excited by 365 nm. The results display that Er3+ doped YbF3 combined with GQDs by introducing an intermediate medium silica layer has a potential application prospect for ultra-sensitive temperature measuring and detection of Fe3+ ions in the field of bio-medicine.

ce:section.ce:para: Monitoring temperature change takes the responsibility of guiding scientific study, industrial production and bio-medical treatment [1,2]. For example, the environmental temperature of cancer cell is often higher than normal cells, real-time temperature detection is an effective method for disease diagnosis [3]. Based on this, ratiometric optical thermometers have been extensively researched due to their advantages of rapid response, high spatial resolution and independence on local environment. The fluorescent thermometers’ design often makes use of various materials such as quantum dots [4], organic dyes [5], polymers as well as lanthanide-doped upconversion nano-/micro-particles (Ln-UCNPs/UCMPs) [6–8]. Among them, the latter excited at 980 nm is considered as a promising candidate for their chemical stability, narrow band emissions,absence of photobleaching and blinking, low toxicity and deep penetration ability [9–11]. Generally, fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) technology in Ln-UCNPs/UCMPs is related to thermal-response two thermal coupled levels (TCLs) from emitter, namely, Er3+ ions (2H11/2/4S3/2) [12], Tm3+ ions (3F2,3/3H4) [13], Ho3+ ions (3K8/5F3) [14] and Nd3+ ions (4F5/2/4F3/2) [15]. Besides, it is known that the high sensitivity (S = dFIR/dT) represents excellent thermometric behaviors, however, low energy gaps (200 cm−1 ≤ ΔE ≤ 2000 cm−1) of TCLs further restrict the enhance of sensitivity and signal discriminability. To avoid the limit of ΔE and spectra overlap of emission bands, non-thermal coupled levels (Non-TCLs) are proposed to gain superior temperature property, such as, Li et al. utilized the Non-TCLs (2H11/2/4F9/2) to calculate absolute sensitivity which was higher than previous reported Er3+ doped materials [16,17].

Iron (Fe) as an essential element in human bodies exists in many enzymes, proteins and some biochemical processes. Therefore, Fe3+ concentration change can also be used to diseases diagnosis, such as kidney damage, anemia and Alzheimer’s disease. Even related study presents the relationship between Fe3+ concentration and cancer [18,19]. Compared with other methods, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [20] and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [21], the fluorescent probe is widely used to detect Fe3+ ions for their low cost, high sensitivity and convenience. At present, metal-organic framework (MOF) and organic dyes play critical roles in the detection of Fe3+ [22,23]. However, their size and low stability at high temperature have limited their application in biomedicine as a fluorescent probe. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as novel zero-dimensional carbon materials with a single-layer carbon core, have attracted considerable interests for its small size distribution, good chemistry stability, unique optical properties. In light of absence of metal elements and its honeycomb structure, GQDs possess low toxicity and good thermal stability with large surface area and are widely used in fields of photodynamic therapy (PDT), photochemotherapy and bioimaging [24–26].

To meet the various requirements in different fields, multifunctional materials are constructed by combining with different functional products. In this work, YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs nano-composites are prepared and show dual-mode applications. The effect of energy back transfer (EBT) process on UC fluorescent property and temperature sensitivity are also discussed based on Er3+-Yb3+ or single Er3+ doped GdF3/YF3/YbF3 nano-/micro-phosphors. On the one hand, it is clearly observed that Er3+ doped YbF3 possesses high thermal sensitivity based on non-thermal coupled levels (4F9/2/4S3/2) of Er3+ ions due to strong EBT process. On the other hand, GQDs exhibit excellent property of Fe3+ detecting. This work provides a new direction for the realization of initial disease diagnosis by nano-composites.

The nano/micro-fluoride crystals were prepared by hydrothermal methods. The raw materials included nitrate hydrate Ln(NO3)3·6H2O (Ln = Yb, Gd, Y and Er), cloride hydrate LnCl3·6H2O (Ln = Yb, Gd, Y and Er), rare earth oxide (Yb2O3(99.99%), Gd2O3(99.99%), Y2O3(99.99%) and Er2O3 (99.99%)), NH4F, NaF and NaBF4 (A.R.) as fluorine sources, citric acid, urea, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O), aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS). The synthesis processes of GdF3, YF3 and YbF3 nano-phosphors were given as follows, and the experimental conditions of micro-phosphors were described in Supplementary Material.

Synthesis of Er3+-Yb3+ co-doped YbF3 nanophosphors: A total of 2 mmol of YbCl3·6H2O, ErCl3·6H2O (ratio:Yb/Er = 1:99) were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water (DI). Simultaneously, 2 mmol of NaF were injected into 10 mL DI. Above mentioned solution was mixed up under stirring for 1 h, and then transferred into 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave to react for 20 h at 180 °C. Finally, the white precipitates were collected by centrifugation and washed three times with DI and ethanol in turn, and further dried at 60 °C for 10 h.

Synthesis of Er3+-Yb3+ co-doped GdF3 nanophosphors: The GdF3: Er3+, Yb3+ phosphors were produced by same process, except for NaBF4 as fluorine sources and YbCl3·6H2O, ErCl3·6H2O and GdCl3·6H2O as reactants.

Synthesis of Er3+-Yb3+ co-doped YF3 nanophosphors: The 2 mmol of rare earth oxide (Yb2O3(99.99%), Y2O3(99.99%) and Er2O3 (99.99%)) were dissolved in 10 mL diluted HNO3 by continuous stirring and heating until evaporating excess nitric acid to obtain transparent RE(NO3)3 solution. And 0.4445 g NH4F injected into 70 mL DI were added to above solution under stirring for 1 h. The subsequent process was similar to that of YbF3.

Synthesis of GQDs: Firstly, the 1 mmol citric acid and 3 mmol urea were dissolved into 20 mL DI, and then transferred into 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave to react for 4 h at 180 °C. At last, the product was obtained after adding ethanol and centrifuged at 10000/10 min.

Synthesis of YbF3: 3% Er3+@SiO2@GQDs: At first, the 0.1 g YbF3: 3% Er3+ phosphors, 80 mL ethanol and 20 mL DI were mixed up ultrasonically for 10 min. The 2.0 mL ammonia aqueous (25 wt%) and 0.2 mL TEOS were dropped into above solution under continuous stirring for 5 h. After centrifuging and washing the precipitations three times with water and alcohol, the YbF3: 3% Er3+@SiO2 products were obtained. Modifying the positive-charged amino group (–NH2) on the surface of samples, the 0.6 g of YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2 were ultrasonically dispersed in 40 mL ethanol for 10 min, followed by dropping 0.12 mL APTMS with stirring for 12 h. Finally, the 20 mL of GQDs solution were mixed up with 0.0138 g YbF3: 3% Er3+@SiO2-NH2 by continuous stirring for 1 h. After that, YbF3: 3% Er3+@SiO2@GQDs samples were collected by centrifuging, washing, and drying at 70 °C for 2 h.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a Rigaku-Dmax 2500 diffractometer at a scanning rate of 15°/min in the 2θ range from 10° to 80°, with graphite monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of the samples were measured by JEM-2100F electron microscope. The Yb3+/Er3+ co-doped LnF3 (Ln = Gd, Y and Yb) samples were filled in an iron sample cell and the temperature of the sample was increased from 298 K to 573 K heated by resistive wire elements. A copper-constant thermocouple buried in the sample was used to monitor the sample’s temperature with a measurement error of ±1.5 K.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Up-conversion luminescence properties and temperature sensing performance
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 (a) exhibits the synthesized process of Er3+ and Yb3+ co-doped LnF3 (Ln = Gd, Y and Yb) nano-/micro- phosphors by one-step hydrothermal method. The pure phases of a series of fluorides are measured by XRD as shown in Fig. 1(b-g). All diffraction peaks match well with corresponding standard data (JCPDS no.12-0788, JCPDS no. 23-1431, JCPDS 49-1805), identifying the successfully introducing Er3+ and Yb3+ ions into LnF3 host. In the view of similar ionic radius (r) of Er3+ (r = 0.81 Å), Gd3+ (r = 0.94 Å), Y3+ (r = 0.89 Å) and Yb3+ (r = 0.86 Å), the Er3+ and Yb3+ as doped ions should occupy Ln3+ sites in LnF3 host lattice. The morphology and size distribution of prepared samples are further displayed in Fig. 1. It can be found that the GdF3 nano-host is peanut shape with the length about 300 nm and width about 100 nm (See Fig. 1h), for micro-phosphors, ellipse shape is observed with size of about 1 μm (See Fig. 1i). YF3 nano-phosphors possess regular cube shape with the size of about 300 nm (See Fig. 1j), micro-phosphors’ non-uniform spherical shape and the size of about 1 μm are shown in Fig. 1k. Simultaneously, YbF3 nano-/micro phosphors belong to irregular cube with the size about 200 nm and 1 μm, respectively (See Fig. 1l and m). In summary, the nano-/micro-LnF3 phosphors are successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method.

To investigate the characteristic of UC luminescence, Fig. 2 (a) and (b) show the UC emission spectra of 1% Er3+, 5% Yb3+ co-doped GdF3/YF3 and 1% Er3+ doped nano-/micro YbF3 phosphors upon 980 nm excitation. Typical Er3+ emissions, both green (2H11/2/4S3/2 → 4I15/2) and red (4F9/2 → 4I15/2) are observed in all phosphors. When doping Er3+ and Yb3+ ions into GdF3 and YF3 nano/micro- phosphors, green emission intensity is always higher than red emission, which can also be observed from integrated emission intensities in insets of Fig. 2(a-b). With increasing Yb3+ or Er3+ doping concentrations, the intensities of red and green emission firstly increase and then reduce gradually when the optimum doped value is reached due to concentration quenching effect [27,28], the optimum doping concentrations of Er3+ and Yb3+ in LnF3 (Ln = Gd, Y and Yb) nano-/micro phosphors are shown in Fig. S1 and S2. In contrast, 1% Er3+ doped nano-/micro YbF3 phosphors all show strong red emission and weak green emission. This phenomenon is mainly ascribed to cross relaxation (CR) processes between Er3+ and Er3+ ions (4F7/2 + 4I11/2 → 24F9/2 or 4S3/2 + 4I15/2 → 4I9/2 + 4I13/2) and energy back transfer (EBT) process (4S3/2 + 2F7/2 → 4I13/2 + 2F5/2) between Er3+ and Yb3+ ions as shown in Fig. 2(c) [29–31].

It is well known that the CR and EBT processes play an important role in enhancing red emission owing to the increase of population at 4F9/2 level and depopulation of 2H11/2 and 4S3/2 levels. Generally, Dexter’s energy transfer theory describes that the probability of energy transfer is related to the distance 1/Rθ between two ions. Thus, the probability of CREr-Er and EBTEr-Yb can be written as [32]:
(1)
P
CR
∝ 1 /
R
Er - E r
θ
(2)
P
EBT
∝ 1 /
R
Er - Y b
θ
Where,
R
Er - E r
and
R
Er - Y b
are average distance of Er3+ and Er3+ ions and Er3+ and Yb3+ ions. θ = 6, 8, 10 represent dipole–dipole, dipole-quadrupole and quadrupole–quadrupole interactions, respectively. The
R
Er - E r
and
R
Er - Y b
can be expressed as:
(3)
R
Er - E r
≈ 2
3 V
4 π
C
Er
N
1 / 3
(4)
R
Er - Y b
≈ 2
3 V
4 π
C
Er + Y b
N
1 / 3
Where, V is the volume of the unit cell, CEr and CEr-Yb are doping concentration of Er3+ and total doping concentrations of Er3+ and Yb3+, and N represents the number of molecules in the unit cell. For GdF3, YF3 and YbF3 nano-/micro-phosphors, the doping concentration of Er3+ is fixed at 0.01, the value of N is 4 and the volumes of the unit cell are 202.34 Å3, 191.42 Å3 and 186.94 Å3, respectively. According to Eq. (3), the calculated average distance Er3+-Er3+ in the GdF3, YF3 and YbF3 host is almost same value, so the effect of CR process can be neglected. In addition, in YbF3 host, Yb3+ ions are not only used to form crystal structure, but also acted as the sensitizer in UC process, thus the total doped concentrations of Er3+ and Yb3+ ions are always fixed at 1. The optical total doping concentrations of Er3+ and Yb3+ in GdF3 and YF3 phosphors are 0.11 and 0.06, respectively. As a result, the REr-Yb of YbF3 is much shorter than that of GdF3 and YF3 phosphors (See Table 1 ), suggesting that high Yb3+ concentration can lead to the reduction of REr-Yb and cause the enhancement of EBT probability based on Eq. (4). The EBT process increases the population in 4I13/2 state by decreasing the population at 4S3/2 state, and then population of 4F9/2 is further populated by energy transfer process (4I13/2 + 2F5/2 → 4F9/2 + 2F7/2). More clearly, the strong red emission and weak green emission are ascribed to EBT process.

Due to the effect of EBT process on population distribution of 4S3/2, the optical thermometric properties of LnF3 phosphors based on 2H11/2/4S3/2 thermally coupled levels are investigated. It is known that the value of R is variable with the temperature change, namely,
R =
R 0
1 + α Δ T
, where, α is linear thermal expansion coefficients and R0 is average distance at room temperature. The values of α of GdF3, YF3 and YbF3 are calculated to be 16.85, 18.7 and 16.34 (10−6/K) by chemical bond theory for complex crystals shown in Tables S1 and S2 (See Supplementary Material), which is so small that REr-Yb of YbF3 is always smaller than that of GdF3 and YF3 with the increase of temperature. Fig. 3 (a) shows the UC spectral mapping of the studied samples at different temperatures. With the increase of temperature, the emission intensities of 2H11/2/4S3/2 → 4I15/2 decrease gradually in all samples, the fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) based on TCLs obeys Boltzmann distribution, namely [33]
(5)
FIR =
I H
I S
=
ħ
ω H
N H
A H
ħ
ω S
N S
A S
=
ω H
g H
A H
ω S
g S
A S
exp
-
Δ E
K B
T

The population ratio of TCLs is given as [34]:
(6)
N H
N S
=
g H
g S
exp
-
Δ E
K B
T

Where the integrated intensity, Planck’s constant, population of each thermalized level, angular frequency, degeneracy and spontaneous emission rate are symbolized by I, ħ , N, ω, g and A.
Δ E
and KB denote energy gaps between TCLs and Boltzmann’s constant, respectively. Owing to depopulation of 4S3/2 state by EBT, the population redistribution which is excited from lower level (Er3+: 4S3/2) to upper level (Er3+: 2H11/2), are blocked with evaluating temperature. The variation trend between FIR values and temperature (T) of LnF3 (Ln = Gd, Y and Yb) nano-/micro-phosphors are shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c), and the ΔE values of GdF3, YF3, YbF3 nano-sized samples are calculated to be about 796 cm−1, 852 cm−1 , 692 cm−1 and those of micro-sized phosphors are about 686 cm−1, 767 cm−1, 595 cm−1. In the view of above results, the plot of FIR and T of YbF3 phosphors with high standard deviation are not perfectly fitted by Eq. (5) and corresponding ΔE is very small, the strong EBT process should be responsible for the abnormal population redistruction between TCLs. The absolute sensitivity (Sa ) and relative sensitivity (Sr ) are used to evaluate thermometric capability, which are defined as the variation of FIR with T by following equation [35]:
(7)
S a
=
dFIR
dT
= F I R
Δ E
K B
T 2
(8)
S r
=
1
FIR
dFIR
dT
=
Δ E
K B
T 2

As plotted in Fig. 3(b) and (c), it is found that the maximum Sa value of nano-sized GdF3, YF3 and YbF3 are around 0.0028 K−1, 0.0025 K−1, 0.0019 K−1 at high temperature and those of micro-sized samples are around 0.0034 K−1 at 498 K, 0.0022 K−1 at 498 K, 0.0017 K−1 at 423 K. Additionally, the Sr values of GdF3, YF3 and YbF3 nano-/micro materials reach the maximum around 0.012 K−1/0.009 K−1, 0.013 K−1/0.011 K−1 and 0.010 K−1 /0.008 K−1 at 303 K. Above results indicate that sensitivity value of YbF3 is smaller than GdF3 and YF3, which certify the effect of strong EBT process on population distribution between 4S3/2 and 4F9/2 in YbF3 phosphors. Moreover, the FIR (I527/I543 ) value based on TCLs is recorded by five heating-cooling cycles from 353 K to 498 K, exhibiting good repeatability of proposed thermometer as shown in Fig. S3 and S5. Temperature accuracy δT as another crucial parameter is used to characterize the smallest temperature change of thermometer, which is defined as [10]:
δ T =
1
S r
δ F I R
FIR
Where δFIR/FIR as a relative error of temperature measurement parameter depends on acquisition setup, its value is usually fixed at 0.5%. The minimum δT of Er3+ or Yb3+ codoped LnF3 (Ln = Gd, Y and Yb) nano-/micro-phosphors are 0.46 K/0.47 K, 0.43 K/ 0.42 K and 0.52 K/0.54 K, respectively (See Fig. S4 and S6).

According to EBT process between Er3+ and Yb3+, the 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 and 4S3/2 → 4I15/2 transition are selected as thermometric parameters. The temperature-dependent behaviors of Er3+ or Yb3+ doped LnF3 (Ln = Gd, Y and Yb) are shown in Fig. 4 (a)and S7(a). With increasing the temperature, the emission intensity of red (4F9/2 → 4I15/2) drops more slowly than that of green (4S3/2 → 4I15/2). Therefore, for non-thermal coupled levels, the plot of FIR (IF/IS ) and T is fitted by e-exponential function with:
(9)
FIR =
I F
I S
=
1
A + B exp ( - C / T )
Where, IF and IS are denoted the integrated intensities of 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 and 4S3/2 → 4I15/2 transitions. A, B and C are constants, T is temperature. According the definition of sensitivity, corresponding absolute sensitivity (Sa ) and relative sensitivity (Sr ) are written as:
(10)
S a
=
dFIR
dT
=
B exp ( - C / T )
A + B exp ( - C / T )
2
C
T 2
(11)
S r
=
1
FIR
dFIR
dT
=
B exp ( - C / T )
A + B exp ( - C / T )
C
T 2

On the one hand, the FIR (I655/I543 ) of YbF3 samples can be well fitted by Eq. (9) with r2 > 0.98 compared with GdF3 and YF3. Meanwhile, calculated sensitivities of YbF3 are higher than those of GdF3 and YF3 phosphors (See Fig. 4(b) and S7(b)). The Sa values of nano-sized and micro-sized YbF3: Er3+ samples showupward trend with the temperature and are as high as 0.127 and 0.097 K−1 at 498 K and good signal discriminability of ~112 nm. The Sr values gradually decrease with the increase of temperature and reach maximum around 0.46% at 303 K. The outstanding repeatability and smallest δT based on Non-TCLs are also investigated and further certify the excellent thermometer performance of YbF3 (See Fig. 4(c–e) and S7(c)). One the other hand, the temperature sensing properties of YbF3: Er3+ have been improved relative to previous reported other thermometric materials as shown in Table 2 . Above result indicates that the YbF3: Er3+ is a promising material for optical thermometer. Taking into account the effect of Er3+ concentration on temperature sensing, we select YbF3: 3%Er3+ materials as researched samples in the next part (See Fig. S8) [35].

To achieve temperature measuring and ion detecting, the new structure, YbF3: 3%Er3+ combined with GQDs as nanocomposites, is designed. The synthetic procedures of nanocomposites are schematically exhibited in Fig. 5 (a), namely, hydrothermal preparation of YbF3: 3%Er3+ precursor, coating silica layers on the surface of prepared nanoparticles, and then obtaining positive charge YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2–NH2 by mixing APTMS, finally modification of YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2 by electrostatic conjunction negative-charged GQDs (See Fig. S9) [42]. The XRD pattern of core–shell structured YbSG matches well with standard card of YbF3 (JCPDS no. 49–1805) with high crystallinity and no diffraction peaks from SiO2 and GQDs are observed due to amorphous silica shell and small amount of GQDs (see Fig. 5(b)). To investigate the morphology and size of prepared samples, Fig. 5 also displays TEM images of YbF3: 3%Er3+, GQDs, YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2 and YbSG with different magnifications, respectively. The precursor is made up of irregular nanoparticles with average size of about 200 nm (Fig. 5(c)). And original prepared GQDs are about 3.7 nm in size (Fig. 5(d)). In Fig. 5(e), it is clear that the average size of the composites increases to about 470 nm, indicating middle silica layers with the thickness of about 80 nm. Finally, GQDs are clearly detected in the silica layer of YbSG with the size of about 4 nm as shown in Fig. 5(f), the lattice fringes of 0.24 nm is indexed to the interplanar spacing of the (100) diffraction facet from HRTEM image (Fig. 5(g)), suggesting that the YbSG nanoplatforms are successfully constructed. In Fig. S10, the samples, YbF3: 3%Er3+, YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2 and YbSG, exhibit similar emission bands. The emission intensities of YbF3: 3%Er3+, YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2 gradually decrease in turn because thicker silica shell weakens the excitation and emission intensities [43]. Comparing with YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2, the emission intensities at 543 nm and 655 nm of YbSG are almost unchanged, indicating that GQDs with a wide broad absorption band ranging from about 300–500 nm do not influence the intensity.

Monitoring accurately temperature and Fe3+ ions content change in cells and tissues is highly necessary for biomedicine, suggesting that effective nano-sensor should be developed in aqueous solution. In our work, the monodispersed YbSG nanocomposites are designed, in which core YbF3: 3%Er3+ and shell GQDs provide noncontact physiological temperature and Fe3+ ions sensing, respectively (See Fig. S11). The experiment device for measurement is displayed in Fig. 6 (a). Firstly, we fit temperature dependence of FIR measured in 655 nm and 543 nm by Eq. (9) in the range of 20–60 °C, the parameters A, B and C are determined to be 0.68, −0.89 and 118.34 (See Fig. 6(b)). The Sa and Sr values are as high as 0.211 K−1 and 0.011 K−1 with small δT (0.40 K) and good repeatability, showing potential application of YbF3: Er3+ in biological field (See Fig. 6(c-e)). Besides, the temperature-dependent FIR (I655/I543 ) of YbF3: 3%Er3+, YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2 are measured. The Sa values of two samples reach as high as 0.261 and 0.193 K−1, which are in the same order of magnitude with the value of YbSG. The results further indicate that SiO2 and GQDs have no effect on sensitivity and YbSG is suitable for non-contact temperature measurements in biotechnology (See Fig. S12). In addition, under the 365 nm excitation, blue emission of GQDs is clearly observed, which is assigned to the electron transition from excited state (π*) to ground state (π*) of the sp2 sites [44]. Then, based on relative fluorescence quenching effect of different metal ions, shell GQDs as a fluorescent probe can be utilized to detect ions by monitoring the change of fluorescence intensity in mixed solution. Corresponding optical photos of solution containing different metal ions Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+ and Zn2+ are shown in Fig. 6(f) under natural light, 365 nm and 980 nm. It can be found serious quenching of blue emission and no obvious changes of red emission for Fe3+ ions, while other ions show almost unchanged for red and blue emission (see Fig. 6(g) and (h)). It can be explained that the Fe3+ ions have large absorption for UV range and no adsorption in long-wavelength red region. Fig. S13 exhibits the UV-absorption spectra of involving different ions in YbSG solutions, in which UV-adsorption of production decreases dramatically in the presence of Fe3+ and further suppresses blue emission. To show the sensing ability of YbSG for metal ions, different concentrations of Fe3+ ions are added to YbSG solution and fluorescence intensities are observed in Fig. 7 (a) and (b). It can be found that the blue emission intensity quenches gradually with adding Fe3+ ions concentrations and red emission remains almost unchanged. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of prepared sample is calculated by Stern-Volmer equation [45]:
(12)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
C
Where F0 and F represent fluorescent intensity of YbSG solution in absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, KSV denotes the quenching constant and C is the concentration of Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) value can be obtained by
3 S D /
K
SV
to assess the efficiency of fluorescent sensor, where SD is standard deviation. The good linear relationship with correlation coefficient square of 0.989 and SD of 0.03436 are shown in Fig. 7 (c) when Fe3+ is at low concentration. The quenching constant (KSV ) and detection limit (LOD) are calculated to be
1.55 ×
10 4
M−1 and 6.65 μM, respectively. According to the change of fluorescence intensities in the presence of specific metal ions under UV light excitation, comparing with the other reported fluorescent probes, GQDs nanocomposites show higher KSV and relative low LOD, suggesting that our samples possess excellent sensitivity for Fe3+ ions detection (See Fig. 7(d)) [45–50]. It is well known that content of Iron element in huamn bodies is closely related to various diseases, e.g. anemia, Alzheimer’s disease, kidney demage and even cancer. Therefore, the YbSG nanocomposites as a novel material is in favor of detecting accurately Fe3+ concentrations by fluorescent quenching of GQDs. Its biocompatibility is also investigated in Fig. 7(e), cell viability is more than 90% at the high dosage of 500 μg/mL, which exhibits minimal toxic effects for cells. We further study the temperature sensing behaviors of YbSG in Fe3+ solutions, in which the sensitivity is almost the same as the value obtained in aqueous solution (See Fig. S14). Hence, YbSG can not only provide temperature measurements but also achieve Fe3+ ions detection, which has potential application in disease diagnosis.

In summary, we successfully prepared Er3+ doped LnF3 (Ln = Gd, Y and Yb) UC nano-/micro-phosphors by hydrothermal method. Their UC spectra are investigated in detail under 980 nm excitation. It can be observed that red emission in YbF3 samples is always stronger than that in GdF3 and YF3 phosphors, attributing to energy back transfer (EBT) process between Er3+ and Yb3+ ions. By Dexter’s energy transfer theory, the probability of EBT in YbF3 materials is higher than other samples due to short distance (R) between Er3+ and Yb3+ ions. With increasing the temperature, REr-Yb in LnF3 is almost unchanged due to small thermal expansion coefficients. Therefore, EBT plays a significant role in the population distribution of 2H11/2, 4S3/2 and 4F9/2 of Er3+. For further developing all-in-one detector, we design a new candidate (YbF3: 3%Er3+@SiO2@GQDs) for temperature sensing based on FIR (I655nm /I543nm ) technology and Fe3+ ion detection by quenching blue emission of GQDs. The maximum absolute temperature sensitivity is 0.211 K−1 at 333 K with good signal discriminability. And Fe3+ ion concentrations are measured at low concentration (0–100 μM). This work not only finds the relationship between EBT process and thermometric ability for highly sensitive thermometer but also exhibits new way for detecting Fe3+ ion concentrations.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.11874182), Science and Technology Project of the 13th Five-Year Plan of Jilin Provincial Department of Education (No. JJKH20190179KJ and JJKH20190766KJ).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Aslandaş, N. Balcı, M. Arık, H. Şakiroğlu, Y. Onganer, K. Meral, Liquid ni-trogen-assisted synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Blueberry and their performance in Fe3+ detection, Appl. Surf. Sci. 356 (2015) 747–752.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A luminescent sensor based on a Zn(ii) coordination polymer for selective and sensitive detection of NACs and Fe3+ ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, X.R. Zhuang, N.X. Zhang, C.Y. Ge, X. Luo, J.X. Li, J. Wu, Q.F. Yang and R.Liu,A luminescent sensor based on a Zn(ii) coordination polymer for selective and sensitive detection of NACs and Fe3+ions, CrystEngComm 21(2019) 1948-1955.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High sensitivity label-free detection of Fe3+ ion in aqueous solution using fluorescent MoS2 quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ma, H.H. Yu, X. Jiang, Z.Z. Luo, Y. Zheng, High sensitivity label-free detection of Fe3+ ion in aqueous solution using fluorescent MoS2 quantum dots, Sens. Actuators B 281 (2019) 989-997.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel multi-functional Pr3+: Bi12SiO20 luminescent nano-sensor for latent human prints, iron ions in drinking water and anti-counterfeiting application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. R. Mahmoud, M. Saif, R. Fouad, Novel multi-functional Pr3+: Bi12SiO20 luminescent nano-sensor for latent human prints, iron ions in drinking water and anti-counterfeiting application, J. Alloys Compd. 805 (2019) 887-895.
